title: Breathless and blue after a dental procedure in a man on dapsone

chart.cc: Shortness of breath and blue discoloration
chart.oneLiner: 34M with type 2 diabetes and a skin condition recently started on dapsone presents with dyspnea, pleuritic chest tightness, headache, and dusky blue lips and fingertips a few hours after a dental procedure with topical numbing spray; an SpO2 of 85% does not rise on oxygen.

vitals.SpO2: 85% on room air (unchanged on supplemental O2)

opening: I can't seem to catch my breath, my chest feels a bit tight, and my lips and fingers have turned a strange bluish-gray color.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 34-year-old man, mildly anxious, dusky/cyanotic appearance but speaking in full sentences. Cooperative.

HPI: Several hours ago he had a dental procedure with a topical numbing spray (benzocaine) applied generously to his gums and throat; he later took a couple of extra over-the-counter throat-numbing lozenges at home for soreness. Over the next 1–2 hours he developed a headache, lightheadedness, fatigue, and shortness of breath, and noticed his lips and fingertips turning a bluish-gray color (reveal the exposure history only if asked about recent procedures/medications/sprays). He also feels a vague tightness in his chest that is a little worse with a deep breath, but NO crushing pain, NO radiation, NO sweating. He is short of breath but is NOT in severe respiratory distress. He recalls the nurse said his oxygen reading was low but he 'looked too blue for the number.' NO cough, NO fever, NO hemoptysis. NO calf pain or leg swelling, NO recent long travel, NO recent surgery, NO prior clots (reveal these VTE-risk negatives only if asked). He has had no relief from the oxygen mask the paramedics placed.
PMH: type 2 diabetes (diet- and metformin-controlled); a chronic inflammatory skin condition (dermatitis herpetiformis) for which a dermatologist started him on DAPSONE about three weeks ago; mild seasonal allergies. He has never had his G6PD enzyme checked. FAMILY: a cousin reportedly 'turned blue' once with a medication, and an uncle of Mediterranean background was told he had 'a blood enzyme problem' (reveal only if asked about family history). MEDS (reveal individually if asked): metformin; DAPSONE (started ~3 weeks ago); occasional ibuprofen; today the dental benzocaine spray and OTC numbing lozenges; a cetirizine antihistamine in spring. Denies nitrates, recreational nitrites ('poppers'), well-water exposure, or recent antibiotics like trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole — reveal only if asked. SOCIAL: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, no recreational drugs, works in an office.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: comfortable-appearing but visibly dusky/cyanotic, alert and oriented, no acute distress.
- Vitals: BP 126/79, HR 106, RR 23, 37.1°C, SpO2 85% that does NOT rise with supplemental oxygen.
- Skin/lips: central cyanosis — grayish-blue lips, perioral and fingertip discoloration; mild scleral pallor; warm and well-perfused (NOT cold/clammy) if examined.
- Nails/extremities: bluish nail beds; brisk capillary refill, no clubbing, no edema; calves soft and NON-tender, no cords, no asymmetry (argues against DVT) if examined.
- Lungs: clear bilaterally, no crackles, wheeze, or consolidation.
- Cardiac: mildly tachycardic, regular, no murmur, no rub; no right-sided heave or loud P2.
- Neuro: alert and oriented, no focal deficit; mild headache only.
- Notable bedside clue (only if asked about blood appearance / venous sample): the blood drawn looks chocolate-brown and does not turn red on exposure to air.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the benzocaine spray, the extra numbing lozenges, the dapsone, or the family history unless asked about recent procedures, the specific medications, or family history.
- Do NOT volunteer the VTE-risk negatives (no calf pain/swelling, no travel, no surgery, no prior clots) unless the relevant question is asked.
- The chocolate-brown blood, central cyanosis, bluish nails, scleral pallor, and the saturation-that-won't-correct are revealed only if specifically examined or asked.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.ABG_cooximetry: Co-oximetry: methemoglobin 32% (markedly elevated; normal <1–2%), oxyhemoglobin ~66%, carboxyhemoglobin <2% (excludes CO). Measured PaO2 95 mmHg (NORMAL) yet the device-measured functional SpO2 is ~85% — the saturation gap (calculated SaO2 from PaO2 would be ~97%). Clinches methemoglobinemia.
labs.ABG_basic: Arterial blood gas: pH 7.38, PaCO2 37, PaO2 95 mmHg, HCO3 22, lactate 2.6 mmol/L (mildly elevated from impaired O2 delivery, not shock); dissolved oxygen is normal and the drawn blood is chocolate-brown.
labs.SpO2_gap: Pulse oximeter reads ~85% and is unresponsive to 100% supplemental O2 (saturation gap = measured SpO2 ~85% well below the ~97% SaO2 calculated from a normal PaO2 of 95); pulse ox falsely plateaus near 85% in methemoglobinemia.
labs.CBC_smear: Hgb 11.4 (down from a baseline of 14.6 three weeks ago), reticulocytes elevated, WBC normal, Plt normal; smear shows bite cells and Heinz bodies — oxidative hemolytic anemia, a SECOND dapsone-driven problem distinct from the dyshemoglobinemia.
labs.Hemolysis_panel: LDH elevated 410 U/L, haptoglobin low/undetectable, indirect (unconjugated) bilirubin 2.4 mg/dL — confirms active hemolysis; direct antiglobulin (Coombs) negative (non-immune, oxidative).
labs.Troponin_BMP: Troponin negative (no ischemia). BMP: glucose 168, creatinine 1.5 (up from baseline 0.9 — mild AKI, likely from hemolysis/volume), K 4.4, normal otherwise.
labs.G6PD: G6PD activity NORMAL on today's quantitative assay (important before methylene blue, which can precipitate severe hemolysis if G6PD-deficient); note an assay drawn during brisk hemolysis can be falsely normal, so interpret with caution.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: The patient's OWN tracing: sinus tachycardia at ~106 bpm with normal axis and intervals; NO S1Q3T3, NO right bundle branch block, NO anterior (V1–V3) T-wave inversion or right-heart strain pattern, and NO acute ischemic ST-T changes — actively argues AGAINST the competing massive-PE/right-heart-strain fork and against ACS.
ekg.verify: From the LITFL ECG library — source a sinus-tachycardia tracing WITHOUT right-heart strain or ischemia matching: sinus tachycardia ~106 bpm, normal axis/intervals, no S1Q3T3, no RBBB, no anterior TWI, no ST changes. Self-host with credit to litfl.com; do not reuse a prior tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing sinus tachycardia without right-heart strain or acute ischemia
searchTerms: sinus tachycardia no right heart strain ECG
sinus tachycardia normal axis no S1Q3T3 12-lead
ekg.clinicalVignette: A 34-year-old man with breathlessness and bluish discoloration after exposure to oxidizing agents has a fast but otherwise unremarkable tracing — no right-heart strain to suggest a large pulmonary embolism.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray (PA)
cxr.findings: Essentially clear lungs — no consolidation, effusion, edema, pneumothorax, or focal oligemia (a clear film with profound cyanosis and a low SpO2 that won't correct on oxygen points away from a primary cardiopulmonary cause and toward a dyshemoglobinemia).
cxr.verify: Pick a CC normal/near-normal PA chest film (the teaching point is a clear film despite cyanosis and a low pulse-ox reading).
cxr.imageDescription: normal PA chest radiograph, clear lungs
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 45-year-old woman presents with cyanosis and dyspnea that fail to improve with supplemental oxygen.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis) and name at least three drugs or oxidizing agents that can cause acquired methemoglobinemia.
steps.rubric: Methemoglobinemia (leading & CANT-MISS,8; accepts acquired/drug-induced methemoglobinemia from benzocaine and/or dapsone). Acute pulmonary embolism — the genuine competing cause of acute dyspnea with hypoxemia and tachycardia that must be excluded (CANT-MISS,4). ACS / other primary cardiopulmonary cause — pneumonia/pneumothorax (core,2). Carbon monoxide / other dyshemoglobinemia such as sulfhemoglobinemia (core,2). Cyanotic congenital heart disease / right-to-left shunt (core,1). Names cyanosis + low SpO2 unresponsive to oxygen after an oxidizing-agent exposure as the key pattern (core,4). Names ≥3 oxidant etiologies of acquired methemoglobinemia — e.g. benzocaine/local anesthetics, dapsone, nitrates/nitrites & poppers, sulfa drugs (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole), phenazopyridine, well-water nitrates (core,4). PENALTY: anchoring on PE/ACS and missing the dyshemoglobinemia clues (chocolate blood, saturation gap) (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Methemoglobinemia (acquired) — driven by BOTH today's benzocaine and the recently started dapsone. The genuine competing diagnosis to exclude is acute pulmonary embolism (acute dyspnea, pleuritic tightness, tachycardia, hypoxemia); also consider ACS and other primary cardiopulmonary causes (pneumonia, pneumothorax), carbon monoxide / other dyshemoglobinemia (sulfhemoglobinemia), and cyanotic congenital heart disease / right-to-left shunt. The discriminating pattern is cyanosis with a low SpO2 that does NOT correct on oxygen plus chocolate-brown blood. At least three oxidant causes of acquired methemoglobinemia: (1) benzocaine and other local anesthetics, (2) dapsone, (3) nitrates/nitrites including amyl-nitrite 'poppers' — and others such as sulfa drugs (TMP-SMX), phenazopyridine, and well-water/food nitrates.

criticalActions.item: Acute pulmonary embolism (competing cause of acute hypoxemic dyspnea to exclude)

coreActions.item: Cyanotic congenital heart disease / right-to-left shunt
Names cyanosis + low SpO2 unresponsive to oxygen after an oxidizing-agent exposure
Names ≥3 oxidant etiologies of acquired methemoglobinemia (benzocaine/local anesthetics, dapsone, nitrates/nitrites/poppers, sulfa drugs, phenazopyridine, well-water nitrates)

penalties.item: Anchors on PE/ACS and misses the dyshemoglobinemia clues (chocolate blood, saturation gap)

mustNotMiss: Acute pulmonary embolism (competing cause of acute hypoxemic dyspnea to exclude)
acceptableAlternatives: Cyanotic congenital heart disease / right-to-left shunt

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Co-oximetry / methemoglobin level (CANT-MISS,5; the only test that confirms and that distinguishes metHb from CO/sulfHb). Arterial blood gas with MEASURED (not calculated) oxygen saturation, looking for a saturation gap (3). CBC with smear + hemolysis labs (LDH, haptoglobin, bilirubin, retic) given dapsone — looks for the concurrent oxidative hemolytic anemia (3). EKG and CXR to exclude the competing PE/cardiopulmonary cause (2; consider D-dimer/CTPA only if PE remains a real concern). G6PD level before treatment with methylene blue (2). BMP for renal function (1). Recognizes pulse ox is unreliable and plateaus near ~85% in methemoglobinemia (bonus,2). PENALTY: relying on the calculated O2 sat from the ABG analyzer (which reads normal) to exclude hypoxia (-3); irradiating with CTPA before recognizing the dyshemoglobinemia (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: ABG cooximetry (methemoglobin level — confirmatory); ABG basic with a MEASURED O2 saturation to demonstrate the saturation gap; the SpO2 gap finding itself; CBC with peripheral smear plus a hemolysis panel (LDH, haptoglobin, indirect bilirubin, reticulocytes) given dapsone; Troponin and BMP; G6PD before methylene blue; and EKG + CXR to exclude the competing PE/cardiopulmonary cause (reserve D-dimer/CTPA for if PE genuinely persists after the metHb is explained).

coreActions.item: ABG with measured O2 saturation (saturation gap)
CBC with smear + hemolysis labs (LDH, haptoglobin, bilirubin, retic) given dapsone
EKG and CXR to exclude the competing PE/cardiopulmonary cause

bonusActions.item: Recognizes pulse oximetry is unreliable (plateaus near ~85%) in methemoglobinemia

penalties.item: relying on the calculated O2 sat from the ABG analyzer to exclude hypoxia
rushing to CTPA before recognizing the dyshemoglobinemia

optionalTests: ABG with measured O2 saturation (saturation gap)
CBC with smear + hemolysis labs (LDH, haptoglobin, bilirubin, retic) given dapsone
EKG and CXR to exclude the competing PE/cardiopulmonary cause
contraindicatedTests: relying on the calculated O2 sat from the ABG analyzer to exclude hypoxia
rushing to CTPA before recognizing the dyshemoglobinemia
orderSensitiveActions: relying on the calculated O2 sat from the ABG analyzer to exclude hypoxia

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings and what they mean for your competing differential.
steps.rubric: This is the patient's OWN tracing. Findings = sinus tachycardia (~106 bpm) with NO right-heart strain and NO acute ischemia. CREDIT: identifies rate & rhythm — sinus tachycardia (6); states the pertinent NEGATIVES — no S1Q3T3, no RBBB, no anterior T-wave inversion / right-heart strain, no acute ischemic ST-T changes (5); links the absent strain pattern to making a large PE less likely and excluding ACS (2); systematic read (2). PENALTY: over-calling ischemia/STEMI or inventing a right-heart-strain/PE pattern (-3).
keyFindings: no S1Q3T3, no RBBB, no anterior T-wave inversion / right-heart strain (argues against a large PE)
no acute ischemic ST-T changes (excludes ACS)
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia with no right-heart-strain pattern (no S1Q3T3/RBBB/anterior TWI) and no acute ischemia — argues against the competing massive-PE and ACS forks.
commonMistakes: over-calling ischemia / attributing the cyanosis to a STEMI
inventing an S1Q3T3 / right-heart-strain pattern to fit a PE
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia at ~106 bpm with normal axis and intervals. Crucially, there is NO S1Q3T3, NO right bundle branch block, NO anterior (V1–V3) T-wave inversion or other right-heart-strain pattern, and NO acute ischemic ST-T changes. On the patient's own tracing these pertinent negatives argue against a large/submassive pulmonary embolism and against ACS, helping tip the fork toward a dyshemoglobinemia as the cause of the cyanosis.

criticalActions: Recognizes: sinus tachycardia with no right-heart-strain pattern (no S1Q3T3/RBBB/anterior TWI) and no acute ischemia — argues against PE/ACS.
coreActions: no S1Q3T3 / RBBB / anterior T-wave inversion (no right-heart strain — argues against a large PE)
no acute ischemic ST-T changes (excludes ACS)

penalties.label: over-calls ischemia / a STEMI, or invents a right-heart-strain/PE pattern

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = essentially clear film. CREDIT: correctly identifies no consolidation/effusion/edema/pneumothorax/focal oligemia (6); notes the clinically important mismatch — a clear film with profound cyanosis and a low SpO2 that won't correct on oxygen points away from a primary cardiopulmonary cause and toward a dyshemoglobinemia (4). PENALTY: inventing an infiltrate or edema (-2).
keyFindings: notes the mismatch — clear film with cyanosis/hypoxemia unresponsive to O2 points to a dyshemoglobinemia
criticalFindings: Essentially clear — no consolidation, effusion, edema, pneumothorax, or focal oligemia.
commonMistakes: inventing an infiltrate or pulmonary edema to explain the cyanosis
steps.idealAnswer: Essentially clear lungs — no consolidation, effusion, edema, pneumothorax, or focal oligemia; a clear film with profound cyanosis and a low SpO2 that won't correct on oxygen points away from a primary cardiopulmonary cause and toward a dyshemoglobinemia.

criticalActions: Recognizes: Essentially clear — no consolidation, effusion, edema, pneumothorax, or focal oligemia.
coreActions: notes the mismatch — clear film with cyanosis/hypoxemia unresponsive to O2 points to a dyshemoglobinemia

penalties.label: invents an infiltrate or pulmonary edema

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Co-oximetry shows a methemoglobin level of 32% with a normal measured PaO2 of 95 mmHg, an SpO2 of 85% that did not correct on oxygen, chocolate-brown blood, COHb <2%, and a normal G6PD. The CBC shows Hgb 11.4 (down from 14.6) with bite cells, Heinz bodies, a low haptoglobin and a high LDH, and the creatinine is 1.5. What is your diagnosis, what concurrent problems do you identify, and what did you rule out?
steps.rubric: Acquired methemoglobinemia from combined oxidant exposure — benzocaine PLUS dapsone (6). Identifies the SECOND, concurrent problem: dapsone-induced oxidative (non-immune) hemolytic anemia (bite cells/Heinz bodies, low haptoglobin, high LDH, Coombs-negative) and the mild AKI as a separate issue (2). Explains the saturation gap (low SpO2 with normal PaO2), the chocolate-brown blood, and the failure of oxygen to correct; names the discriminating tests; rules out the competing PE/ACS via the strain-free EKG, clear CXR, and normal PaO2, and rules out CO via the low COHb; notes residual caution that a G6PD assay drawn during hemolysis can be falsely normal (2).
steps.idealAnswer: Acquired methemoglobinemia driven by combined oxidant exposure — today's benzocaine on top of recently started dapsone. Concurrent problems to call out as separate issues: (1) dapsone-induced oxidative hemolytic anemia — Hgb fallen to 11.4 with bite cells and Heinz bodies, low haptoglobin, high LDH and a negative Coombs (non-immune); and (2) a mild AKI (creatinine 1.5). The diagnosis is confirmed by the saturation gap — a low SpO2 with a normal measured PaO2 — the chocolate-brown blood, and the failure of oxygen to correct the saturation. I ruled out the competing acute PE / ACS using the strain-free, ischemia-free EKG, the clear CXR, and the normal PaO2, and ruled out carbon monoxide via the low COHb. One residual caveat: a G6PD assay drawn during active hemolysis can be falsely normal, so methylene blue is given with caution and close monitoring.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including disposition and what you would counsel before any safe discharge?
steps.rubric: Per MGH toxicology/dyshemoglobinemia guidance: stop and remove ALL offending oxidizing agents — hold the benzocaine AND the dapsone (CANT-MISS,5); methylene blue 1–2 mg/kg IV over 5 min for symptomatic methemoglobinemia or level >20–30%, repeat once if needed (CANT-MISS,6); confirm/consider G6PD status before methylene blue and know it can be falsely normal during hemolysis (4); supportive care including supplemental O2 even though SpO2 won't fully correct, IV fluids for the hemolysis/AKI, and monitor for rebound metHb from dapsone's long half-life — consider multiple-dose activated charcoal (3); toxicology / Poison Control consult and admit/monitor serial methemoglobin and Hgb levels (2); safe-discharge plan — med reconciliation removing the oxidant, document a drug/oxidant-avoidance list and a dapsone allergy/caution, patient education on warning signs, ensure metHb normalized off therapy with no rebound and hemolysis/renal function stabilized, dermatology follow-up for an alternative to dapsone, and outpatient G6PD retesting after recovery (5). PENALTY: giving methylene blue to a (truly) G6PD-deficient patient — can worsen hemolysis (-3); discharging or treating as simple hypoxemia / escalating oxygen alone while ignoring methemoglobin (-3); continuing dapsone (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Stop and remove ALL offending oxidizing agents — discontinue the benzocaine products AND hold dapsone. Give methylene blue 1–2 mg/kg IV over 5 minutes for this symptomatic methemoglobinemia (level 32%, >20–30%); repeat once if the level or symptoms persist. G6PD tested normal, but it can be falsely normal during active hemolysis, so dose with caution and watch closely; if methylene blue is contraindicated or refractory, use ascorbic acid, exchange transfusion, or hyperbaric oxygen. Provide supplemental oxygen and supportive care; give IV fluids for the oxidative hemolysis and mild AKI; because dapsone has a long half-life, anticipate REBOUND methemoglobinemia and consider multiple-dose activated charcoal and a longer monitoring window with serial methemoglobin and hemoglobin levels. Consult toxicology/Poison Control and admit. Before any safe discharge: reconcile medications and document the oxidant-avoidance list and a dapsone caution; counsel the patient on warning signs (returning blue color, breathlessness, dark urine); confirm the methemoglobin has normalized off therapy with no rebound and that hemolysis and renal function have stabilized; arrange dermatology follow-up for a dapsone alternative and outpatient G6PD retesting after recovery; ensure close primary-care follow-up.

criticalActions.item: Methylene blue 1–2 mg/kg IV for symptomatic/severe methemoglobinemia
Stop / remove ALL offending oxidizing agents (benzocaine AND hold dapsone)

coreActions.item: Confirm/consider G6PD before methylene blue (and know it can be falsely normal during hemolysis)
Supportive care: supplemental O2, IV fluids for hemolysis/AKI, monitor for rebound metHb (dapsone long half-life), consider multi-dose activated charcoal
Toxicology / Poison Control consult and admit with serial methemoglobin and Hgb levels

Safe-discharge plan: med reconciliation/oxidant-avoidance list and dapsone caution, patient education on warning signs, confirm metHb normalized with no rebound and hemolysis/renal stable, dermatology follow-up for a dapsone alternative, outpatient G6PD retesting

bonusActions.item: Alternatives if methylene blue contraindicated/refractory (ascorbic acid, exchange transfusion, hyperbaric O2)

penalties.item: Giving methylene blue to a truly G6PD-deficient patient
Treating as simple hypoxemia / escalating oxygen alone while ignoring methemoglobin

requiredActions: Methylene blue 1–2 mg/kg IV for symptomatic/severe methemoglobinemia
Stop / remove ALL offending oxidizing agents (benzocaine AND hold dapsone)
optionalActions: Confirm/consider G6PD before methylene blue (and know it can be falsely normal during hemolysis)
Supportive care: supplemental O2, IV fluids for hemolysis/AKI, monitor for rebound metHb (dapsone long half-life), consider multi-dose activated charcoal
Toxicology / Poison Control consult and admit with serial methemoglobin and Hgb levels

Safe-discharge plan: med reconciliation/oxidant-avoidance list and dapsone caution, patient education on warning signs, confirm metHb normalized with no rebound and hemolysis/renal stable, dermatology follow-up for a dapsone alternative, outpatient G6PD retesting
Alternatives if methylene blue contraindicated/refractory (ascorbic acid, exchange transfusion, hyperbaric O2)

steps.disposition: Admit and monitor serial methemoglobin and hemoglobin levels; discharge only after metHb normalizes off oxidants with no rebound and hemolysis/renal function stabilize

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 34M with type 2 diabetes and dermatitis herpetiformis recently started on dapsone presents with dyspnea, pleuritic chest tightness, headache, and dusky blue lips and fingertips hours after a dental procedure with topical benzocaine. The diagnosis is acquired Methemoglobinemia from COMBINED oxidant exposure (benzocaine plus dapsone), reached by eliciting the focused exposure and medication history, performing the targeted exam (central cyanosis, chocolate-brown blood, an SpO2 that won't correct on oxygen, non-tender calves), and interpreting the directed workup — a saturation gap with elevated methemoglobin on co-oximetry — while excluding the genuine competing acute PE/ACS via the strain-free EKG, clear CXR, and normal PaO2. A concurrent dapsone-induced oxidative hemolytic anemia and a mild AKI are recognized as separate problems.
keyHistoryClues: Topical benzocaine spray and OTC numbing lozenges hours before onset
Dapsone started ~3 weeks ago for a skin condition (a second oxidant)
Cyanosis and low SpO2 that do not improve with supplemental oxygen
Chocolate-brown venous blood that does not turn red on air exposure
No calf pain/swelling, travel, surgery, or prior clots (lowers the competing PE)
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Skin/lips: central cyanosis — grayish-blue lips, perioral and fingertip discoloration; mild scleral pallor; warm and well-perfused (NOT cold/clammy).
Nails/extremities: bluish nail beds; brisk capillary refill, no clubbing, no edema.
Calves soft and NON-tender, no cords, no asymmetric swelling (argues against DVT and against the competing PE).
Lungs: clear bilaterally, no crackles, wheeze, or consolidation.
Neuro: alert and oriented, no focal deficit; mild headache only.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals — profound cyanosis with an SpO2 of 85% that is unchanged on oxygen.
Elicit the focused history, drawing out the recent benzocaine exposure, the recently started dapsone, the VTE-risk negatives, and family history only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal central cyanosis, bluish nail beds, scleral pallor, non-tender calves, clear lungs, and chocolate-brown blood.
Build a differential led by Methemoglobinemia while explicitly excluding the genuine competing acute PE and ACS, and name ≥3 oxidant etiologies.
Order a focused workup — co-oximetry/methemoglobin level, an ABG with measured oxygen saturation, and hemolysis labs — and interpret the strain-free EKG, clear CXR, and the saturation gap.

Commit to Methemoglobinemia from combined benzocaine+dapsone oxidant stress, recognize the concurrent oxidative hemolysis and mild AKI, and manage with removal of ALL oxidants (including dapsone), methylene blue (after checking G6PD), supportive care with rebound monitoring, toxicology consult, admission, and a structured safe-discharge plan.
commonPitfalls: Giving methylene blue to a truly G6PD-deficient patient (and not knowing the assay can be falsely normal during hemolysis)
Trusting the calculated O2 saturation from the ABG analyzer (which reads normal) and missing the saturation gap
Anchoring on PE/ACS and rushing to CTPA before recognizing the dyshemoglobinemia
Escalating oxygen alone for an SpO2 that physiologically cannot correct
Continuing dapsone or missing the concurrent oxidative hemolytic anemia

Stop / remove ALL offending oxidizing agents — benzocaine AND dapsone
Anticipate rebound methemoglobinemia from dapsone's long half-life and monitor accordingly

Essentially clear — no consolidation, effusion, edema, pneumothorax, or focal oligemia.

Stop / remove ALL offending oxidizing agents (benzocaine AND hold dapsone)

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
